coredata.dc:description: Textile dyeing industrial wastewater poses significant environmental risks due to diverse pollutants, specifically vibrant colors and the stable structure of dye molecules in the water bodies. Therefore, effective recycling strategies, including advanced oxidation or physicochemical methods, are required for the efficient remediation. This work presents a metal-free pNCQDs@rGA photocatalyst, synthesized via a single-step hydrothermal process, addressing limitations of metal-based counterparts. The 3D rGA network facilitated charge separation and transport, enhancing photocatalytic activity. Fabric integration ensured recyclability without causing secondary pollution. The photocatalyst achieved complete methylene blue degradation within 80 minutes, with holes and hydroxyl radicals identified as primary active species. This facile synthesis and effective performance highlight the potential of pNCQDs@rGA for wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Textile dyeing industrial wastewater poses significant environmental risks due to diverse pollutants, specifically vibrant colors and the stable structure of dye molecules in the water bodies. Therefore, effective recycling strategies, including advanced oxidation or physicochemical methods, are required for the efficient remediation. This work presents a metal-free pNCQDs@rGA photocatalyst, synthesized via a single-step hydrothermal process, addressing limitations of metal-based counterparts. The 3D rGA network facilitated charge separation and transport, enhancing photocatalytic activity. Fabric integration ensured recyclability without causing secondary pollution. The photocatalyst achieved complete methylene blue degradation within 80 minutes, with holes and hydroxyl radicals identified as primary active species. This facile synthesis and effective performance highlight the potential of pNCQDs@rGA for wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing environmental challenges posed by industrial wastewater, particularly from textile dyeing processes have driven the need for innovative and efficient treatment solutions [1,2]. The effluents from textile industries contain various synthetic dyes that are higher molecular weight toxic chemicals including residual dyes that pose significant risks to soil and water quality [3]. The concentration of these dyes in textile wastewater typically ranges from 2 % to 50 % depending on the dye class used in dyeing and printing processes. The amount of azo dyes are used in textile industries are approximately 80 % and 10–15 % of these dyes are discharged into the environment as effluent that is washed off from fabric [4]. This untreated wastewater leads to water quality degradation and disturbs the aquatic ecosystem. The persistent nature of synthetic dyes makes them particularly dangerous for aquatic life by causing toxicity, disrupting ecosystems, and promoting bioaccumulation while posing serious health issues to humans [5–7]. This contaminated water leads to waterborne diseases, and long-term exposure to toxic chemicals also degrades the water quality, affecting biodiversity and economic activities [8].

Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) for wastewater treatment were further categorized into different methods. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) represent a group of techniques targeting the removal of organic pollutants [9,10]. Process selection and integration depend upon specific effluent characteristics [11]. UV/H2O2; this process achieved complete dye degradation within 20 minutes and a 63 % total organic carbon (TOC) reduction within 90 minutes [12]. Combination of TiO2/UV/H2O2; under 110-minute irradiation, dye degradation efficiencies were observed as follows: UV (18 %), UV-H₂O₂ (27 %), UV-TiO₂ (37 %), and UV-TiO₂-H₂O₂ (64 %) [13]. Photocatalysis via nano-photocatalysts; nano-photocatalysis is an emerging technology, facilitates dye mineralization. This process demonstrates the potential for 50–80 % degradation of micro pollutants under UV light irradiation [14]. Au-TiO₂/SiO₂ catalyst, effectively degrade dye stuff from wastewater in the presence of NaBH₄, and photodegrade 95 % of Rhodamine B dye in 20 minutes under visible light irradiation [15]. However these techniques often fall short in efficiently removing dye stuff due to high cost, utilization of limited UV-Visible light range and environmentally unsustainable [16]. Therefore, need an advanced material and catalytic systems that offer both highly efficient, abundant and cost-effective route to address this global issue. About 200 semiconductors have been reported as suitable photocatalysts for this purpose [17–19]. In recent decades, photocatalysts used for the degradation of organic dyes were TiO2 nanoparticles, bismuth compounds and carbon nitride. TiO2 nanoparticles favors the reduced band gap and enhancing the absorption of visible light [20]. However, metal oxides are not attractive due to several drawbacks, such as metal ion leaching over time, electron trapping by metal centers, and scarcity of metals [21]. Bismuth compounds considered as a promising photocatalyst due to its exceptional intrinsic features and narrow band gap (2.4 eV). But the low quantum efficiency due to the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs as in other semiconductors catalysts [22]. Later, the utilization of carbon materials specifically, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is currently emerging. CQDs are low-cost and plentiful alternatives providing an extensive tunability of properties in recent times, capable of photosensitization applications. However, the fast recombination rates of CQDs are one of the existing bottlenecks, which bring a great obstacle in their photocatalysis. Additionally, owing to the greater dispersion of CQDs in water, it is difficult to recycle the CQDs from liquid phase reactions. Addressing the challenge of enhancing catalytic performance and recovery efficiency remains an ongoing concern [23]. Since 2009, graphene was initially employed as a co-catalyst to enhance TiO2 photo-activity, in order to extend current state of progress within the subject of “graphene & photocatalysis” study. However, 2D graphene-based materials face different challenges as poor dispersion, restacking, and multilayer thickness have been recognized as barriers to practical applications. Additionally, issues related to difficult separation from water sources further limit their practical application [24].

One promising approach lies in the design and application of 3D hierarchical catalyst structures, which combine various functional materials to enhance adsorption, degradation and reusability [25]. Knitted Kevlar fabric, known for its exceptional strength, durability and stability even at high temperature proposed a versatile platform for the growth of advanced catalytic materials. When coupled with graphene aerogels and partially nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (pNCQDs), this composite structure can serve as a highly efficient catalyst for textile effluent treatment [26]. The nitrogen atom, as a dopant comprises five electrons in a valence shell and a comparable size to the atom required to bond with carbon. N-doped CQDs have better photocatalytic activity, due to the formation of a p − n junction structure, with the n-type nature originating from nitrogen-rich domains and the p-type nature induced by oxygen functionalities. The regions of p- and n-conductivity are responsible for producing H2 and O2 gas, respectively [27]. Nitrogen-free QDs with p-type conductivity only catalyze H2 evolution when illuminated, suggesting that the band bending in the p-type sections aids in electron injection for H2 production [28]. The unique properties of 3D graphene aerogel including surface area and mechanical stability make it a suitable support for photocatalysis.

In this present research work, we developed an effective partial nitrogen-doped CQDs (pNCQDs@rGA) photocatalyst for the reduction of cationic dye specifically methylene blue (MB) as a target pollutant of textile effluent, using 3D-porous GA substrate under visible light irradiation. To suppress the rapid charge recombination in CQDs, the synthesis process was carried out in the presence of ammonia and oxygen-rich domains without using ammonia. A junction structure is expected to form via such a process, resulting in better charge separation CQDs with p-n junction structure were directly formed on the surface of GA, to ensure the ease of recovery from treated water (H2O). As ammonia (nitrogen source) is a volatile species and can be removed by evaporation, nitrogen contents are not available during the formation of surface structure. Because π–π interaction in pNCQDs was effectively immobilized onto the graphene network hydrothermally. With the synergistic effects arising from pNCQDs and GA, the obtained (pNCQDs@rGA) catalyst demonstrated superior photodegradation of MB in its solution and can easily recover.

Graphite powder having an average (particle size) of 20 μm was purchased from SK Chemical Daejeong CO., Ltd (Siheung-si, South Korea). The chemicals utilized for the synthesis of pNCQDs@rGA, including 99 % sulfuric acid (H2SO4), 99 % potassium permanganate (KMnO4) powder, 35 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), 35 % hydrochloric acid (HCl), 30 % ammonia (NH3) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Saint Louis, USA). Wet-spun non-woven polyester fabric, single jersey weft knitted Kevlar fabric, and plain-woven carbon fabric with an arial density of 110 g/m², 200 g/m², and 250 g/m², respectively, were purchased from the local market. The fiber diameter of polyester, Kevlar, and carbon substrate was 10 μm, 12 μm, and 5 μm, respectively.
Analytical-grade chemicals were used for the synthesis of pNCQDs@rGA without purification. The distilled water was utilized in this study.

The Modified Hummer’s method was used for the synthesis of graphene, as reported in the literature [27]. Briefly, purified graphite powder (5 g) was stirred in H2SO4 (99 %) till the graphite hunks were entirely converted into small particles. Furthermore, 25 g of KMnO4 (99 %) was added gradually to the dispersion of graphite powder placed in the ice-bathed setup, so that the solution temperature was maintained below 10 °C. After it, the continuous stirring for 16 hours at 60 °C where the color turned dark brown. Afterward, 500 ml of deionized water was poured into the solution to lower the temperature by about 5 °C, to prevent further oxidation. The resulting dispersion of graphene oxide sheets was bright yellow, after the addition of 10–20 ml of H2O2. Later, for the removal of acids and metal contamination from the obtained solution was washed with HCl solution, acetone, and distilled water respectively. The separation of unreacted graphite from the GO, obtained dispersion was centrifuged at 4000 rpm. The GO dispersion was then further dialyzed for one week with a dialysis membrane having a molecular weight of 14 kDa, to achieve the neutral pH. The purified solution of GO was used for the synthesis of pNCQDs on graphene aerogel (GA).

GO solution was used for the in-situ growth of CQDs on 3D GA through a one-pot hydrothermal process, followed by the freeze-drying method. In short, a homogeneous mixture was acquired via ultrasonication of GO to form (1 %) w/v solution for 20 min. After GO dispersion, 0.5 g of citric acid (CA) and 0.4 ml of NH3 were further mixed in solution. Where after, the prepared solution was ultrasonicated to make a homogenous solution before being sealed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and temperature retained at 140 ℃ for 4 h. This initial hydrothermal treatment results in the formation of nitrogen-doped domains, serving as nucleation sites for the CQDs growth. After this reaction, NH3 was evaporated to get a neutral pH and then restarted the hydrothermal reaction for 3 h under the same conditions. The solution concentration was kept the same as before by adding distilled water. Furthermore, 0.5 g CA was also added in the second time hydrothermal treatment, which forms an undoped carbon structure. After hydrothermal treatment, GA modified with partially nitrogen doped CQDs (pNCQDs@rGA) was collected using the freeze-drying process for 48 h. The resultant materials were denoted as pNCQDs@rGA. For comparison, N-doped CQDs at graphene aerogel (NCQDs@rGA) and undoped CQDs at graphene aerogel (UCQDs@rGA) were also synthesized with the only difference in the methodology that completing the 4 h hydrothermal procedure without eliminating the NH3 and without using NH3 in the hydrothermal reaction, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of pNCQDs@rGA growth on textile substrates
ce:section.ce:para: For this purpose, the addition of textile substrate took place in the reactor along with other precursors. During the hydrothermal process, the GO sheets were thermally reduced and self-assembled to form a 3D porous structure with the synthesis of CQDs. Some remaining hydroxyl groups of GO take part in hydrogen bonding or intermolecular attractions or π-π stacking that ensure the attachment to the textile substrate.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM JEOL JEM-2100 F) was used for the analysis of the size and shape of the synthesized pNCQDs@rGA. The surface morphology of the assembled GQDs was analyzed using a field emission electron microscope (FE-SEM; JEOL JSM 670 F) with an acceleration voltage of 15 kV. Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) spectrum was executed to observe the crystal structure of the pNCQDs in the 2θ range of 100 – 800. The X-ray source of Cu Ka (Rigaku D/MAX-2500) was used at an acceleration voltage of 40 kV manufactured by Rigaku Denki. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.) was used to estimate differences in functional groups. Atomic composition and bonding were investigated via an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Multilab ESCA lab-system VG Thermo-Scientific, USA). Electrochemical cyclic voltammetry testing was used to verify the pn junction system. Photoluminescence excitation (SCINCO) of the synthesized material was observed to check the fluorescence of NCQDs. The absorbance scans of the pNCQDs@rGA were studied using a UV–visible absorbance spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The efficiency of the synthesized product was estimated by photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) dye solution. Calculated amount of aerogel (0.05 g) put into 40 ml solution of MB (40 mg/L) at room temperature. Under dark conditions, the solution was magnetically stirred to achieve adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the catalyst and the dye. Monitored by irradiating to (300 W) Xe lamp, ranging from 450 to 700 nm, by keeping the distance of 20 cm between the light source and solution. The solution was taken out after a consecutive time interval of 20 min up to 80 min and observed by a UV-Visible spectrophotometer to check the intensity of the degradation curve. This equation gives the efficiency of MB degradation: D %= (C
0

0 ) ∗100, where C
0 is the initial concentration and C is the residual concentration of MB after the reaction. The rate constant kinetics equation can be expressed as ln(C 0
/C) = ka ∗t where ka is the rate constant (min−1), C
0 is the initial concentration (mg l−1), and C is the reaction concentration of the MB solution when the irradiation time is zero and t min.

The efficiency of synthesized photocatalysts was also determined by turnover frequency. The Turnover Frequency (TOF) values indicate the intrinsic photocatalytic efficiency of the materials, normalized per active site.

ce:para.ce:italic: TOF= No. of dye molecules degraded/ No. of active sites*time

ce:section.ce:para: Standard method No. 5220 D was used to calculate the COD of textile dyeing effluent before and after treatment. The samples were examined utilizing standard methods to test the quality of water i.e., American Water Works Association (AWWA), American Public Health Association (APHA), and Water Environment Federation (WEF).

Standard method No. 5210 B was used to calculate the BOD of textile dyeing effluent before and after treatment. The samples were examined utilizing AWWA, APHA, and WEF, while the Lovibond Senso Direct 150 was utilized to check the BOD values.
Reduction percentage = (Initial value – Final value / Initial value) * 100

The pNCQDs used for the photocatalysis of MB are controlled by their size-dependent quantum confinement that affects their surface. To observe the photocatalysis of pNCQDs, conducting nano-substrate is crucial to control the self-aggregation of pNCQDs, which can cause less performance. This problem can be successfully addressed through the heterogeneous growth of pNCQDs having a (p-n junction) on conductive nano substrates. Furthermore, the rapid charge recombination in CQDs is also addressed to synthesize the nitrogen-rich domain using ammonia and oxygen-rich domain without using ammonia as a solvent that serves as an electron sink and hole-rich region respectively at the porous rGA. Suppose to synthesize pNCQDs@rGA, the pNCQDs growth mechanism was directed to rGA ( Fig. 1). This type of growth reaction aids the in-situ growth of pNCQDs@rGA and is abbreviated as pNCQDs@rGA. The defect sites at rGA serve as sites of nucleation where the pNCQDs grow heterogeneously. The porous rGA has a large surface area and a passive for the direct growth of pNCQDs. The pNCQDs@rGA composites were previously produced by adsorption, thermal cross-linking, or using other multiple synthesis steps [29,30]. The synthesis of N-doped CQDs using harsh chemicals like hydrazine, followed by decontamination, surface triggering, and cross-linking by chemical or thermal process. It is time time-consuming process for the separation and purification of pNCQDs. Overhead all these ambiguities, the fixation of the synthesized product on the surface of the textile substrate was the major issue. Previously different types of binders were used for this purpose that suppressed the desired activity of the coated textile substrate by making bonds and covering the active sites. This research work illustrated the single-step synthesis by in-situ growth of pNCQDs@rGA as well as the attachment of the as-synthesized product on the textile substrate. We optimized the concentration of the precursors, synthesis temperature, and pH to attain a defined growth of pNCQDs@rGA. The facile growth of pNCQDs@rGA in one step assists the facile fabrication and application of this pNCQDs@rGA for the photocatalytic degradation of MB.

To approve the in-situ growth of pNCQDs@rGA used TEM analysis was used, as shown in Fig. 2. Three-dimensional (3D) distribution of pNCQDs may be examined at rGA, as illustrated under low-resolution TEM analysis. It could be observed from Fig. 2(a) that rGO had highly transparent two-dimensional (2D) layers with apparent wrinkles and bends. Fig. 2(b) demonstrates the wrinkled structure of rGO having pNCQDs, but it is not visualized properly at the resolution of 0.2 µm. At the resolution of 10 nm, Fig. 2(c, d) clarified the distribution of amorphous pNCQDs with a size less than 10 nm and the FFT image, respectively; such spatial distribution of growth confirms the functionalization on the whole surface of rGA. The reaction temperature and the number of reactants have significant effects on the size of pNCQDs@rGA [31].

This unique microstructure can facilitate the extensive range of surface and interfacial properties, which results in the enhancement of the surface area for the better attachment of dye molecules for photocatalysis. Irregular pNCQDs@rGA was observed clearly, but due to the low temperature (140 ℃) synthesis of pNCQDs@rGA could not observe crystal fringes of the pNCQDs@rGA. The FE-SEM was used to further investigate the surface topography of in-situ grown bare rGO & pNCQDs@rGA. Fig. 2(e) presents the SEM images at the resolution of 100 nm and 50 nm of the as-obtained sample, revealing that it consists of bare rGO nano-sheets closely connected to form a 3D porous network. Fig. 2(f) demonstrates the thread-like stranded structure where abundant pores uniformly distributed on the surface can be detected and are highlighted in the image. A specific 3D hierarchical porous structure is revealed due to the mutual effect of partial Nitrogen doping and the CQDs. The addition of functional groups by the growing pNCQDs@rGA leads to suppressing the aggregation and can accommodate a large content on the surface due to their small size. The presence of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional entities [32], reduced a negative charge due to its electronegative nature.

Raman spectrum provides information about structural defects present in pNCQDs@rGA. It is obvious from Fig. 3(a) that the peaks at 1341 cm−1 (D-band) and 1574 cm−1 (G-band) were paralleled to the vibrations of the disordered sp3 hybrid carbon atoms and the ordered sp2 hybrid carbon atoms [29]. Besides, defects on the surface of rGO and pNCQDs@rGA can be guessed by the intensity of ID and IG (D & G band, respectively) [33]. The ID/IG ratios were estimated as 1.00 and 1.06 for bare rGO and pNCQDs@rGA, respectively. These results showed that pNCQDs@rGA had more surface defects than bare rGO. So, the doping of partial N-CQDs by ammonia at rGO can enhance surface defects.

Confirmation has been done to verify the variations in active groups, structure, and conformation of synthesized pNCQDs@rGA. XPS results are also parallel to the oxygenic surface groups determined synthesis of pNCQDs@rGA. Bare rGO retains maximum carbon content, however, pNCQDs@rGA growth caused further functional groups on the carbon network. It is justified from the XPS survey of bare rGO and pNCQDs@rGA in Fig. 3(b) that the specific peak of N1s can be spotted in pNCQDs@rGA. That demonstrates the effective recombination of pNCQDs with rGO. Notably, the intensity (1.66) of C1s to O1s in pNCQDs@rGA is lower than compared of bare rGO (1.73), signifying that the introduction of pNCQDs creates more oxygen-containing functional groups. Similarly, Fig. 3(c, d, and e) respectively, revealed the XPS spectra for C1s, O1s, and N1s. In Fig. 3(c) the C1s spectrum of pNCQDs@rGA expresses peaks at 284.7 eV for C-C, 285.0 eV for C-N, 286.1 eV for C-O, and 288.2 eV for CO. Here, the oxygenated active groups have lower peak intensity as compared to the graphitic C–C/C = C bonds, proposing the elimination of some OH active groups and the conversion of GO into rGO during synthesis. As compared to the rGO peaks, the successful doping of N-atoms into the rGO lattice has been confirmed with the specific C-N peak in the pNCQDs@rGA [34]. Fig. 3(d) demonstrates the three peaks of the N1s spectrum at 398.7 eV for pyridinic N, 399.9 eV for pyrrolic N, and 401.2 eV for graphitic N. These serve as more evidence that the N-atoms generated as defects have been incorporated into the hexagonal (carbon) lattice and can be used as a central dipole.

The content of the animated and oxygenic active groups was linked with pNCQDs@rGA, which can provide an extremely great extent of surface-active groups due to the small size. This factor leads to the creation of an active site for photocatalytic degradation. Fig. 3(e) indicates the deconvoluted O1s spectrum in which three different peaks are located at 532.1 eV for –CO, and 533.7 eV for –COH in graphene [31,32]. The FTIR analysis has been done to assess the presence of relevant active groups. In Fig. 4(a), a band can be observed around 3373 cm−1 that identifies O-H or N-H stretching vibration in rGO and pNCQDs@rGA, respectively [24]. Both samples contain a very small number of O-H groups that correspond to structural OH or might be due to the absorption of atmospheric moisture throughout the analysis or the reduction of COOH groups. However, the peak intensity of active groups of pNCQDs@rGA is greater than compared of rGO, which signifies the reduction of GO efficiently because of enhanced surface area with a decrease in the particle size. A peak at 2891 cm−1 was related to the C-H symmetric stretching in both samples.

The stretching vibration peak of the CO can be seen in both samples, but in the case of pNCQDs@rGA, the peak was much more intense due to the presence of amide linkage. The peak at 1654 cm−1 characterizes the CC stretching in both samples [24,35]. It proposes that the formation of the CC unsaturated bonds in pNCQDs@rGA was comparable to the results of SEM [36]. However, the distinctive absorption band at 1386 cm−1 is the flexural vibration of C-NH [37,38]. And this peak is absent in the case of rGO. It specifies that the ammonia (NH3) as a nitrogen source has been effectively doped into the pNCQDs@rGA. The peak at 1183 cm−1 is linked with the C-O stretching vibration. The bending vibration of N-H is located at 885 cm−1. Hence, these results prove that the surfaces of synthesized pNCQDs@rGA contain amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. It also determined that these pNCQDs@rGA retain better water solubility, high ion affinity, and outstanding optical properties [38]. The crystal structure of pNCQDs@rGA was analyzed using wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WA-XRD). Fig. 4(b) demonstrated the peaks of bare rGO at 23.6° and 43.8° paralleled to the characteristic (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes, respectively while in pNCQDs@rGA, the intensity increased slightly in both planes This result was attributed to the coexistence of (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of both pNCQDs and rGO. Additionally, the (0 0 2) plane peak of pNCQDs@rGA shifted towards the lower angle from the higher lattice arrangement of edges, the reason behind this was the (mutual) repulsion between negatively charged active groups of pNCQDs@rGA [39].

In Fig. 4(c), the peak emission intensity of rGO was observed to be the highest among the investigated materials, indicating an elevated recombination rate of photo-generated carriers in rGO. The emission peak intensity of UCQDs@rGA was lower compared to pure rGO, while the emission peaks of pNCQDs@rGA were significantly diminished in comparison to rGO alone. These findings suggest that the incorporation of the carbon-based material led to a reduction in the recombination of photogenerated carriers in CQDs. Upon partial nitrogen doping, a significant portion of electrons generated after the photoexcitation was efficiently directed away by rGO, resulting in a prominent decrease in the possibility of electron recombination with holes. Consequently, the emission peak intensity of CQDs in the pN-doped sample was lower than that of UCQDs and rGO. The results from photoluminescence spectroscopy emphasized that the conductivity of carbon materials played a crucial role in diminishing the recombination probability of photo-generated carriers. Simultaneously, the combination of carbon materials with CQDs demonstrated an enhanced capacity to facilitate the separation of photo-generated carriers to a certain extent [40]. To determine the optical properties of the synthesized material, UV–visible spectroscopy has been performed. From Fig. 4(d), it was observed that under daylight, the aqueous solution of UCQDs@rGA and pNCQDs@rGA was brownish-golden and bright green under the ultraviolet region. This absorption is due to the aromatic π networks in carbon [34]. In rGO, the disappearance of the oxide groups causes the absorption band to be at 300 nm. This happens because the effect of the CC bond increases after thermal reduction, which increases the sp2 bond [41]. In the case of pNCQDs@rGA exhibited an absorption band aligned at 289 nm is exhibited, which is credited to the (n–π*) transition of the carbonyl group, because the excited state surface energy is trapped, which emits the resilient fluorescence. Therefore, the excitation wavelength was identified at 330 nm, showing that the UCQDs@rGA and pNCQDs@rGA revealed a strong fluorescence emission peak with a difference in intensities. Furthermore, firstly, the fluorescence intensity of pNCQDs increases with the increase in excitation wavelength, then it decreases, as excitation of 330 nm red shift reaches its maximum. According to certain research, carbon dots may have optical characteristics like fluorescent surface groups. Furthermore, produced pNCQDs@rGA showed favorable water solubility and greater immovability at room temperature [29]. To verify the proposed Z-scheme alignment in our photocatalytic system, we investigated the variance in the HOMO-LUMO energy level configurations of the synthesized material pNCQDs@rGA and compared it with UCQDs@rGA. For accurate energy level determination, ferrocene was employed as an external redox standard for electrochemical potential calibration. For pNCQDs@rGA, the HOMO level is positioned at approximately 5.02 eV and the LUMO level at 5.05 eV, while UCQDs@rGA exhibits a higher HOMO at 5.29 eV and a lower LUMO at 5.45 eV as shown in Fig. 4(e, f) calculated by the inset equation EHOMO/LUMO= [(EOX/RED-E1/2(ferrocene)+ 4.8)] eV. This shift in energy levels upon partial nitrogen doping signifies a modulation of the CQDs' electronic properties, likely due to the introduction of nitrogen-related defect states. The lower HOMO energy for pNCQDs@rGA suggests enhanced oxidizing power of photogenerated holes, whereas the higher LUMO (less negative) might slightly affect electron reduction potential [42]. However, the overall improved charge separation facilitated by the nitrogen doping and the conductive graphene aerogel support is crucial. The observed differences in HOMO and LUMO levels directly correlate with the altered redox potentials and band gap energies, providing a fundamental understanding of why partial nitrogen doping leads to enhanced photocatalytic activity by optimizing charge carrier generation and transfer processes at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface.

The photocatalytic degradation activity of the synthesized pNCQDs@rGA was estimated using MB as the target pollutant. For comparison, the photocatalytic performances of NCQDs@rGA and UCQDs@rGA were also tested. A sequence of experiments concerning the effect of N-doping on CQDs@rGA under visible light irradiation time from 0 min to 80 min (with consecutive time intervals of 20 min) was considered. 40 ml MB, having a concentration of 40 mg/l, was treated with 0.05 g of all three types of mentioned aerogels. To observe the adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst, MB dye was kept in the dark for about 40 min. The comparable adsorption of dye [43], i.e., decrease in dye concentration under dark conditions, reflects that our designed aerogel has a comparable surface area. Then, the percentage degradation values of the MB dye were attained after visible light irradiation time of 80 min; 53.8 % for NCQDs@rGA, 93.6 % for UCQDs@rGA, and 98.9 % pNCQDs@rGA, respectively. Irradiation time from 0 min to 80 min, pNCQDs@rGA showed a high degradation rate as shown in Fig. 5(a, b). From Fig. 5(c) the rate constant kinetic was determined as the correlation between ln(C 0
/C) and time. The photo-catalyst has the maximum rate constant (0.18547 min−1), which supports the photodegradation results and demonstrates the catalyst's favorable properties and strong MB degradation activity in the presence of visible light. The reason behind this is the utilization of initial ammonia-rich and finally ammonia-free synthesis conditions. As ammonia is a volatile species that reduces the nitrogen content, the surface became enriched with an oxygen domain and formed a junction. This junction structure indicates that the e-h+ pair recombination is more effectively inhibited than the other two. In NCQDs@rGA, the nitrogen content is present during the whole reaction, as nitrogen species can influence the charge carrier recombination dynamics. If nitrogen groups introduce additional recombination centers, it can lead to quicker recombination of the photo-generated e-/h+, reducing the overall efficiency of the process. In UCQDs@rGA there is a lack of nitrogen, so no junction structure could form. This in-situ doping and growth of CQDs on graphene aerogel as substrate imparts a prominent role in reporting [33] the superior performance of the pNCQDs@rGA, as compared to conventional. To study the influence of synthesis temperature on the (size) dimension and attachment of pNCQDs@rGA, considered three temperature ranges as 140 ℃, 180 ℃, and 220 ℃ [44]. pNCQDs@rGA-140 that were synthesized at 140 ℃ demonstrated better photocatalytic activity of MB than the other two temperature ranges elaborated in Fig. 5(d, e). The largest rate constant of the optimized temperature range 140 ℃ illustrates 0.0845 min-1, which is the maximum for better photocatalytic activity in comparison with others, as shown in Fig. 5(f). The reason behind these results is the growth of smaller-sized and efficient amounts of pNCQDs with better attachment on the surface of rGO aerogel, while in the case of high-temperature ranges, there was the growth of larger-sized and smaller amounts of pNCQDs on the surface of rGO [33,44]. In simple can say there is the fact that smaller-sized pNCQDs are attached in greater numbers on the surface of rGA and illustrate the better photocatalytic activity of MB. As well as to observe that N did not interact with rGA to form N-doped rGA substrates via single step hydrothermal route, compared our optimized photocatalyst pNCQDs@rGA with reduced graphene aerogel (rGA) and nitrogen doped reduced graphene aerogel (NrGA) and its C/C
0 as shown in Fig. 6(g, h). rGA and NrGA exhibit significant MB adsorption capacity attributed to their high specific surface area porous 3D network facilitating dye diffusion, and strong π–π interactions [45]. Nitrogen doping in NrGA further enhances adsorption via induced defects and functional groups. However, both show negligible visible-light photocatalytic activity due to graphene's zero bandgap, causing rapid electron-hole recombination and insufficient photoactive sites for ROS generation, even with nitrogen modification. Negligible degradation of MB has been observed in case of NrGA due to photothermal effect. Conversely, incorporating partially nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots at graphene aerogel (pNCQDs@rGA) introduces discrete bandgaps, enabling visible-light-driven photoactivity and efficient electron-hole pair generation and separation. These photogenerated charges produce ROS, effectively degrading MB [46]. The presence of pNCQDs@rGA slightly reduces adsorption due to partial surface coverage, demonstrating an inverse relationship where enhanced photocatalysis from pNCQDs@rGA compromises some adsorption capacity in the composite system. As well as the Turnover Frequency (TOF) values indicate the intrinsic photocatalytic efficiency of the materials, results demonstrate that partial nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on graphene aerogel possess the highest photocatalytic efficiency (3.01 × 10⁻⁴ mol g⁻¹ s⁻¹), substantially outperforming both fully nitrogen-doped CQDs (2.02 × 10⁻⁵ mol g⁻¹ s⁻¹) and undoped CQDs (3.52 × 10⁻⁵ mol g⁻¹ s⁻¹) as calculated in (S4). This highlights that partial nitrogen doping optimizes the electronic structure and active site accessibility, enhancing charge transfer and reducing recombination, while excessive nitrogen may negatively affect site activity.

To study the effect of concentration of precursors; citric acid (1 g) and ammonia (0.8 g) were liquefied in the GO dispersion (constant) by stirring. To clarify the starring role of pNCQDs@rGA and to adjust the concentrations compared the photocatalysis of the pNCQDs@rGA with varying pNCQDs contents. The increased amount of citric acid and ammonia was selected to attain high N-doping [47]. We optimized the number of precursors at various concentrations of citric acid and ammonia. To analyze the effect of citric acid; 0.5 g (C-CS), 1 g (C-C1), and 2 g (C-C2) of citric acid by keeping ammonia (0.8 ml) constant were used for pNCQDs@rGA synthesis as shown in Fig. 6(a, b). Whereas for testing the nitrogen content of pNCQDs@rGA, we used 0.4 ml (C-A1), 0.8 ml (C-A2), and 1.6 ml (C-AS) of ammonia by keeping citric acid (1 g) constant respectively as shown in Fig. 6(d, e). The higher rate constant of (0.01754 min−1) has been shown by the photocatalyst. It demonstrates that the catalyst has acceptable qualities and significant MB photodegradation under visible light, which is compatible with the photocatalytic degradation results.

Using the aforesaid optimized amount of citric acid and ammonia, relatively enhanced performance was attained, in comparison with other concentrations used. This can be considered as the uniformly distributed pNCQDs@rGA while using optimized concentration. In Comparison, using minimum concentrations of citric acid (0.5 g) the content of pNCQDs@rGA formed is much lower, which shows the lower photocatalytic activity. In the same way, at a higher concentration of citric acid (2 g) the large-sized aggregated pNCQDs@rGA lumps are formed and promote more adsorption processes than photocatalysis. These agglomerates of pNCQDs@rGA hinder the diffusion and interconnections with rGA and affect the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, utilizing the concentration of ammonia 0.8 ml and 1.6 ml affects the formation of porous aerogel [48]. Therefore, using an optimized concentration of citric acid (1 g) and ammonia (0.4 ml), a suitable size of pNCQDs was grown on perfectly formed rGA which boosts the photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic irradiation of MB dye is possible due to the influence of reactive species. To understand the degradation mechanism of methylene blue dye using pNCQDs@rGA; NaCl, EDA, Na2CO3 and EtOH were chosen as a charge scavenger. Based on catalysis results in the presence of scavengers, reactive oxygen species or catalytic pathways predominantly responsible for the degradation process can be identified [30]. By selectivity inhibiting specific reactive oxygen species or altering reaction conditions, the role of each species in the degradation is different; hence catalytic centers can be elucidated for composites. The quenching agent which suppresses the degradation process to the maximum extent is considered the most effective for catalysis. Role of each scavenger is as follows: NaCl: For quenching hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (¹O₂), while influencing ionic strength and electrostatic interactions in the degradation process. EDA: For quenching hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (¹O₂) and superoxide anions (O₂⁻•), while altering the efficiency of the oxidative process. Na2CO3: For quenching hydroxyl (OH), which influences the reactivity of both CQDs and dye. EtOH: For quenching O2 - and electrons and reducing the oxidative degradation of dye. The photocatalytic degradation of MB was figured out at 98.6 % by using the pNCQDs@rGA heterojunction. From Fig. 7(d) this percentage of degradation was decreased to 75 %, 78 %, 83 % and 89 % with aforementioned scavengers as NaCl, Na2CO3, EDA, and EtOH respectively [49]. The percentage of photocatalytic activity was considerably quenched with the addition of NaCl and Na2CO3 which signifies the major role of holes and hydroxyl radicals in this process [30]. Thus, by using pNCQDs@rGA under visible light irradiation, OH radicals and holes are the controlling species in the photocatalytic process.
(1) pNCQDs@rGA + hν → h+ e-
(2) pNCQDs (e-) + rGO → pNCQDs + rGA (e-)
(3) rGA (e-) + O2 → rGA + O2 (radical)
(4) O2 (radical) + H2O →OH (radical) + OH-
(5) h+ + OH- → OH (radical)
(6) h+ + O2 (radical) → OH (radical) + MB → Degradation product

The mineralization efficiency refers to the organic contaminant like a dye molecule is broken into simplest inorganic form i.e.CO2, H2O, and mineral salts. In photocatalysis, it indicates the degradation of dye molecules as well as their complete transformation into harmless inorganic substances. Usually, reactive oxygen species such as •O2 − and •OH radicals are primarily attributed to the degradation of MB. Upon excitation, photogenerated e− and h+ are shifted toward the photocatalyst surface. The e− species reduce molecular oxygen O2 to form superoxide radicals •O2 − whereas h+ oxidizes H2O to produce •OH or oxidizes MB directly [50,51]. These reactive species •O2 −, •OH and h+ drive redox reactions that degrade MB into CO2, H2O, or inorganic ions, resultantly MB becomes colorless as the aromatic rings within dye are degraded. From Fig. S1 (supporting information), the degradation of MB starts with the attack of •OH on C–S+ =C functional group. This initiates the breakdown of MB by disrupting the double bond conjugation and converting the C–S+ =C to C–S (=O)–C. The central aromatic containing S and N opens to maintain the stability. Meanwhile the hole induced H+ plays a key role in forming CH and NH. This molecular cleavage produces smaller, highly oxidized intermediates which are primary cause of dye degradation [52]. The ⋅OH targets the side chain of MB, initiating a demethylation process. The change in color of MB is due to the protonation of the atomic ring and is a reversible process. The formation of free radicals in the photocatalytic process has been facilitated by pH. Four pH levels, 3, 6, 9, and 13, were considered to check the MB photocatalytic activity. In Fig. 7(a, b) it can be seen in the degradation percent equation described above, the photocatalytic efficiency increases to 98.6 % as pH increases. In Fig. 7(c), the rate constant is 0.01369 min−1 for the pNCQDs@rGA at optimized pH 13.

The reason behind this phenomenon could be elaborated as: during the photoreaction, free radicals of OH and O2 were formed, which are the basis of the photocatalytic process. Consequently, more radicals would be formed due to the higher concentration of OH− in an alkaline medium, increasing photocatalysis. Moreover, with increased surface area and mesoporous structure in addition to different active surface groups that contain oxygen pNCQDs@rGA in the 3D matrix not only lessens the recombination of e-/h+ pairs of pNCQDs as well raises the adsorption ability of MB, signifying the synergistic effect between these two factors. Inversely, at examined pH ranges, there was the presence of negatively charged surface species in the synthesized material, whereas the MB structural molecules would liquefy into MB+ ions in an aqueous medium. Here, the presence of electropositive and electronegative interactions and π-π bonding between the dye molecule and material because of the aromatic ring in the structure of the dye molecule and the electron-rich area in the synthesized material. Concerning the acid medium, the formation of H+ ions dominated, which suppressed the immersion of MB at the surface of the material, as there exists the competition of prior adsorption between H+ and MB+ ions, hence blocking the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized aerogel. Additionally, the time to access the point of adsorption-desorption equilibrium has elongated, which was verified by the lower MB adsorption capacity in the basic environment as compared to an acidic environment. Hence, at a low pH level, the photocatalysis of the material compressed although had a large contact area of synthesized rGA [39]. Fig. 9 shows the real-time images of the whole experimental work during the optimization of levels of factors after photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the type of pNCQDs@rGA coated textile substrate for photocatalysis of MB
ce:section.ce:para: After the optimization of all the above-mentioned parameters, we observed the photocatalytic activity of pNCQDs@rGA by applying it to three different types of fabric substrates. The pNCQDs@rGA morphology remains the same grown on fabric or in solution. For comparative analysis considered non-woven fabric (P-GA), knitted Kevlar (K-GA), and woven carbon fabric (C-GA). By following the same optimized hydrothermal procedure, here, in situ development of pNCQDs@rGA at the textile’s surface was achieved without additional binder and the crosslinking agent. It was seen that K-GA showed enhanced photocatalysis due to the knitted 3D network of Kevlar that causes better attachment of pNCQDs@rGA on the surface. On the other hand, C-GA demonstrated less photocatalysis in comparison to carbon fabric, which was plain woven. Due to the fineness and high conductivity of carbon fabric showed good attachment with pNCQDs@rGA and showed better photocatalysis. While in P-GA (non-woven), there is a lack of a 3D network and fineness, so the attachment of pNCQDs@rGA on the surface is not satisfactory, which suppresses the photodegradation activity. The percentage photodegradation of the above-mentioned treated textiles is shown in Fig. 8(a), and the kinetics study and the rate constant of the optimized knitted textile are demonstrated in 8(b, c), respectively. To determine the recyclability of as-prepared optimized fabric (K-GA), it was necessary to achieve the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye in each cycle. Here the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye using K-GA repeated five times [53]. After 5 consecutive photodegradation cycles, no significant reduction in photodegradation efficiency of MB was observed after (optimized) 80 mins of visible light irradiation as shown in Fig.(S2). This demonstrates that the pNCQDs@rGA coated knitted kevlar maintains a significant portion of its activity even after repeated use and preventing clumping and fracturing during repeated use and handling. Its mechanical strength allowed for easy recovery and handling of the photocatalyst after each cycle, minimizing material loss and damage. Furthermore, the strong adhesion of the pNCQDs@rGA to the Kevlar fabric prevented significant catalyst leaching or detachment, which contributes to the maintenance of photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficiency of photocatalyst (pNCQDs@rGA) in real-world effluent conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The photodegradation efficiency of MB has been assessed near about real-world effluent conditions (containing MB dye, NaCl, NaOH, pH (5−6)). Resultantly, the photocatalysis was done in 85 minutes. The images of photodegradation of MB after a time interval of 20 minutes up to 85 minutes are shown in Fig. 9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalysis by using Kevlar fabric@graphene aerogel@pNCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the Kevlar Fabric@Graphene Aerogel@pNCQDs structure, the combination of pNCQDs, rGA, and Kevlar fabric creates a synergistic effect that significantly enhances photocatalytic performance. The p-n heterojunction facilitates efficient charge separation, the graphene aerogel provides a high surface area and electron transport, and the Kevlar fabric offers structural support. This design leads to improved light absorption, reduced charge recombination, and enhanced generation of ROS, resulting in highly efficient degradation of textile effluents like MB. In pNCQDs, the successful e-/h+ pair separation was attained in a p-n type heterojunction. The p-type semiconductors, where holes are the primary charge carriers, and an n-type semiconductor, where electrons are the primary carriers, combine to generate this system. Because an additional electric field is created at this type of heterojunction, it is possible to expedite the migration of e-/h+ pairs and boost photocatalytic performance [25]. When immersed in an aqueous solution, p-n type semiconductors generally form a junction with a spatial charge area at the interface between them as a result of electron and hole diffusion. Electrons flow in the opposite direction to holes due to the electrical potential generated at that junction. In addition to the absorbance spectrum, another parameter that critically affects the photocatalytic activity of the excited electron–hole pair. Furthermore, the knitted Kevlar fabric serves as a robust support to pNCQDs@rGA photocatalyst that allows even dispersion of the active materials, maximizing the amount of the catalyst exposed to the pollutant and light.

This study has demonstrated that synthesized pNCQDs@rGA with the above-optimized factors (temperature, pH, and concentration) were capable of photocatalysis of MB under visible-light irradiation, as these photo-generated e-/h+ pairs undergo redox reaction. The developed pNCQDs@rGA photocatalyst consists of nitrogen-doped CQDs decorated on the graphene aerogel, with oxygen groups at the surface. Therefore, the scavenger test was carried out to investigate the mechanism. NaCl and EDA (scavenger for holes), EtOH (scavenger for O2 - and e-), and Na2CO3 (scavenger for . OH) were used in this study [30]. This percentage of photodegradation decreased from 98.6 % to 75 % and 78 %, respectively, with the addition of NaCl and Na2CO3, which signifies the major role of holes and hydroxyl radicals in this process of photocatalysis of MB.

From the obtained results, a potential mechanism for the photodegradation of methylene blue over pNCQDs@rGA is proposed as shown in Fig. 10. The higher photodegradation of pNCQDs@rGA is due to the synergetic effect of pNCQDs@rGA. An internal electric field is created at the p-n heterojunction interface as electrons move from p to n until their Fermi level achieves equilibrium. Electrons from the valence band to the conduction band are energized when the heterojunction is exposed to radiation. Meanwhile, the h+ moves from the valence band of n-type to the valence band of p-type, so the recombination rate of h+ and e- decreases, resulting in improved photodegradation.

In this study, COD and BOD were done to evaluate the efficiency of synthesized pNCQDs@rGA to treat textile dye effluent to confirm the photocatalysis activity. For this purpose, textile dye effluent was taken from the National Textile University’s (Textile Processing Lab). The hydrochemical analysis of a sample was done. Standard method 5220 D was used to calculate the COD, and for BOD calculation using the standard method 5210 B of a textile dye effluent. The results obtained from this analysis revealed a high degree of COD and BOD of textile dye effluent before treatment, as 1050 mg/l and 750 mg/l, respectively. After treatment of dye effluent with pNCQDs@rGA, the values of COD and BOD showed a significant decrease in the values as 225 mg/l and 31 mg/l, respectively. The calculated reduction percentage was 78.5 % for COD and 95.8 % for BOD, as shown in Table 1. The method had brought the limit of COD to well below 250 mg/l and 30 mg/l for BOD to a safe discharge of effluent into water-bodies [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of synthesized material with the material present
ce:section.ce:para: The comparison of photocatalysis among various carbon materials for the degradation of MB, with degradation efficiency and reaction time, is mentioned in Table 2. GA-CQDs/CNN achieves 91 % degradation in 240 mins while EFB-NCQDs and GA-g-C₃N₄ show improved efficiency at 97 % and 83 % respectively within the shorter reaction time of 180 mins. BMO/GA enhances the degradation to 98.3 % in a significantly reduced time of 110 mins. Whereas, the synthesized pNCQDs@rGA demonstrates the highest efficiency by achieving 98.9 % photodegradation within 80 mins. The results indicate that pNCQDs@rGA exhibits superior photocatalytic activity, which is due to the improved surface area and electron transfer capability attributed to the presence of partial nitrogen doping and reduced graphene aerogel support. Thus, facilitating faster and more efficient MB degradation under visible light. The high efficiency and reduced reaction in the visible region make it a promising candidate for effective wastewater treatment.

This research emphasizes sustainability through the utilization of environmentally friendly precursors and a streamlined, single-step hydrothermal synthesis of the photocatalyst, completed within 4 h at a relatively low temperature of 140 °C. Furthermore, the in-situ single-step hydrothermal fabrication of the photocatalytic textile ensures enhanced reusability, minimizes catalyst leaching into water bodies and delivers efficient photodegradation in a shorter timeframe (80 mins) compared to previously reported methods, contributing to a more sustainable water treatment approach.

This study developed a pNCQDs@rGA photocatalyst, synthesized via a single-step process, for efficient visible-light degradation of methylene blue (MB). The pNCQDs, in situ grown on rGA, exhibited enhanced charge separation due to p-n junction formation and suppressed recombination, validated by TEM, SEM, XPS, FTIR, XRD, Raman, and Photoluminescence analyses. pNCQDs@rGA achieved 98.9 % MB degradation within 80 mins, superior to NCQDs@rGA and UCQDs@rGA, with optimal performance at pH 13. Knitted Kevlar-supported pNCQDs@rGA demonstrated high efficiency due to its 3D network. This robust photocatalyst retained high activity over five cycles, offering a promising solution for textile effluent remediation. This study's limitations include lab-scale synthesis, requiring scale-up for commercial viability. Future work should focus on optimizing the photocatalyst to broaden its absorption spectrum into the UV-Visible range and accelerate dye degradation kinetics.

Mumtaz Ali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Munir Ashraf: Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.
